summary: Gruber reviews Mac OS X 10.5 Leopard on its release day, calling it a worthy update with a high ratio of improvements to regressions. He identifies Time Machine as the most significant new feature, arguing that its gimmicky UI is actually a strength because it motivates people to use backup software they'd otherwise ignore. Beyond Time Machine, he praises the accumulated small refinements — Quick Look, unified window chrome, and countless tiny UI improvements — as what truly makes Leopard better. He notes a few missteps like the translucent menu bar, but concludes that Apple's willingness to go back and polish small details is what keeps Mac OS X improving.
key_points: Time Machine's flashy UI is deliberately gimmicky, but that's a feature not a bug — it gets people to actually use backup software, which saves data that would otherwise be lost.
The argument for Time Machine's design isn't that it's more usable, but that more people will use it, and for backup software that distinction matters more than anywhere else.
Quick Look and the unified window chrome are standout smaller features that quickly become indispensable.

Apple's greatest achievement in Leopard is the accumulation of tiny, unglamorous refinements — rewritten UI copy, smarter file renaming defaults — that won't make headlines but cumulatively improve the experience.
The translucent menu bar is called out as a clear misstep that will look dated by the next release.
money_quotes: Making backup software that people can't wait to try, and which, once activated, just automatically kicks in and does its thing on a regular schedule, is like making people want to go ahead and sign up for life insurance.
The argument for Time Machine's game-like UI isn't that it's more usable, but that more people will use it.
There will be data saved that would otherwise have been lost if Time Machine instead sported a more traditional, straightforward visual appearance.
No one ever got hurt by waiting a week or two to install a new OS.
It takes about 30 seconds of playing with Quick Look to wonder how you ever lived without it.
The design of Time Machine is the single most Apple-like thing in Leopard. No way would a UI like this have come from anyone else.
This isn't just slightly better wording, it's completely better. Same exact feature, but someone at Apple took the time to rewrite the UI copy, and it's better for it.

Leopard is chock full of details like this — little things that won't be promoted on the box cover or mentioned in mainstream media reviews, but which, taken cumulatively, epitomize why Mac OS X keeps getting better with each major release.
key_insight: The best measure of a platform's maturity isn't its headline features but the accumulation of tiny, unglamorous refinements that show someone cared enough to go back and get the details right.
